attrs.alt: Contributor role: what it allows and what its NotActions exclude

content.text: What the Contributor Role Grants

Contributor is the most commonly assigned broad role for teams who build and operate Azure resources. Like Owner, it has Actions: ['*'], granting every control plane operation on every Azure resource provider. The critical difference is in NotActions: Contributor explicitly excludes all Microsoft.Authorization write and delete operations, which means it cannot create or modify role assignments, custom role definitions, or resource locks.

This separation of resource management from access management is intentional. A development team with Contributor can build, deploy, configure, and delete any Azure resource in their scope, but cannot grant that access to others or lock resources against deletion. Access management remains the responsibility of a designated Owner or User Access Administrator.
Role Definition and NotActions

# Inspect the Contributor role definition
Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name 'Contributor' | ConvertTo-Json -Depth 5

# Key fields:
# Id          : b24988ac-6180-42a0-ab88-20f7382dd24c
# Actions     : [ '*' ]   <- ALL control plane operations
# NotActions  : [          <- 6 explicit exclusions
#   'Microsoft.Authorization/*/Delete',
#   'Microsoft.Authorization/*/Write',
#   'Microsoft.Authorization/elevateAccess/Action',
#   'Microsoft.Blueprint/blueprintAssignments/write',
#   'Microsoft.Blueprint/blueprintAssignments/delete',
#   'Microsoft.Compute/galleries/share/action'
# ]
# DataActions : []   <- no data plane access

# The critical NotAction: Microsoft.Authorization/*/Write
# This blocks Contributor from:
#   - Creating role assignments (New-AzRoleAssignment)
#   - Modifying custom role definitions
#   - Creating lock resources (Microsoft.Authorization/locks/write)

# What Microsoft.Authorization/locks/write means:
# Contributor CANNOT create resource locks (ReadOnly or Delete locks)
# because locks are Microsoft.Authorization resources.
# You need Owner or a role with Microsoft.Authorization/locks/write.

# Verify this:
Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name 'Contributor' | Select-Object -ExpandProperty NotActions
NotAction
What It Blocks
Microsoft.Authorization/*/Delete
Cannot delete role assignments, role definitions, deny assignments
Microsoft.Authorization/*/Write
Cannot create/modify role assignments, role definitions, resource locks
Microsoft.Authorization/elevateAccess/Action
Cannot elevate to root management group access
Microsoft.Blueprint/blueprintAssignments/write
Cannot create Blueprint assignments
Microsoft.Blueprint/blueprintAssignments/delete
Cannot delete Blueprint assignments
Microsoft.Compute/galleries/share/action
Cannot share Compute galleries cross-tenant
Assigning the Contributor Role

Contributor is appropriate for teams and automation identities that need to create and manage resources but should not control who has access. Resource group scope is the right level for most teams — it limits the blast radius if credentials are compromised or a misconfiguration is made. Subscription-level Contributor is appropriate only for platform teams managing the entire subscription.

# Assign Contributor at resource group scope — most common pattern
$group = Get-MgGroup -Filter "DisplayName eq 'Backend Team'"
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId $group.Id `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Contributor' `
  -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/backend-prod-rg'

# Assign to a CI/CD service principal
$sp = Get-MgServicePrincipal -Filter "DisplayName eq 'github-actions-prod'"
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId $sp.Id `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Contributor' `
  -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/deploy-rg' `
  -PrincipalType ServicePrincipal

# Contributor + User Access Administrator: the combination that approximates Owner
# Use this only when a team needs both resource management AND access management
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $group.Id -RoleDefinitionName 'Contributor' -Scope $rgScope
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $group.Id -RoleDefinitionName 'User Access Administrator' -Scope $rgScope

# Check what a Contributor can and cannot do
$contrib = Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name 'Contributor'
# Check if locks are allowed
$contrib.Actions -contains 'Microsoft.Authorization/locks/write'  # False
$contrib.NotActions -contains 'Microsoft.Authorization/*/Write'   # True
Assign to security groups rather than individual users so membership changes don't require role assignment changes.
CI/CD service principals should receive Contributor only at their specific deployment resource group, not at subscription scope.
If a team also needs to manage access, combine Contributor with User Access Administrator at the same scope — this matches Owner's effective permissions without using the Owner role name.
Consider PIM-eligible Contributor for production scopes where standing access is a risk.
Resource Locks and the Authorization Gap

A common source of confusion is that Contributor cannot create resource locks. Locks (Microsoft.Authorization/locks/*) are Microsoft.Authorization resources, and Contributor's NotActions blocks all Microsoft.Authorization writes. A Contributor who wants to prevent accidental deletion of a critical resource cannot create a Delete lock. Solutions include a custom role or granting User Access Administrator at the specific resource scope.

# Resource locks require Microsoft.Authorization/locks/write
# Contributor is blocked from this by its NotActions.
# Options when a Contributor team needs to manage locks:

# Option 1: Create a custom role with Contributor + lock management
$contributorWithLocks = @{
    Name = "Contributor with Locks"
    IsCustom = $true
    Description = "All Contributor permissions plus resource lock management"
    Actions = @("*", "Microsoft.Authorization/locks/*")
    NotActions = @(
        "Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments/*",
        "Microsoft.Authorization/roleDefinitions/*",
        "Microsoft.Authorization/elevateAccess/Action",
        "Microsoft.Blueprint/blueprintAssignments/write",
        "Microsoft.Blueprint/blueprintAssignments/delete"
    )
    DataActions = @()
    NotDataActions = @()
    AssignableScopes = @("/subscriptions/<sub-id>")
}
New-AzRoleDefinition -Role $contributorWithLocks

# Option 2: Grant User Access Administrator at the specific resource where locks are needed
# UAA allows Microsoft.Authorization/* so lock management is included
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId '<principal-id>' `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'User Access Administrator' `
  -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/critical-rg/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/criticaldata'

# Check current locks on a resource group
Get-AzResourceLock -ResourceGroupName 'backend-prod-rg'
Contributor for CI/CD Pipelines

CI/CD pipelines are one of the most common Contributor assignment targets. The pipeline service principal needs to create, update, and delete Azure resources as part of each deployment. Using Contributor at the deployment resource group scope — combined with federated credentials for GitHub Actions or Azure DevOps — is the recommended pattern. Where possible, narrow the role to a custom role covering only the resource providers the pipeline actually deploys.

# CI/CD pattern: Contributor + federated credential (no stored secret)

# GitHub Actions workflow setup
# 1. Create the service principal
$app = New-MgApplication -DisplayName 'github-backend-prod'
$sp = New-MgServicePrincipal -AppId $app.AppId

# 2. Add federated credential for GitHub Actions main branch
New-MgApplicationFederatedIdentityCredential -ApplicationId $app.Id -BodyParameter @{
    name = 'github-main'
    issuer = 'https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com'
    subject = 'repo:contoso/backend:ref:refs/heads/main'
    audiences = @('api://AzureADTokenExchange')
}

# 3. Assign Contributor to the SP at the deployment resource group
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId $sp.Id `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Contributor' `
  -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/backend-prod-rg' `
  -PrincipalType ServicePrincipal

# GitHub Actions workflow snippet (no secrets required):
# - name: Azure Login
#   uses: azure/login@v1
#   with:
#     client-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}
#     tenant-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
#     subscription-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}
# - name: Deploy
#   run: az deployment group create --resource-group backend-prod-rg --template-file main.bicep

# Least-privilege refinement: if the pipeline only deploys specific resource types,
# use a custom role with only those resource provider operations instead of Contributor
$deployOnlyRole = @{
    Name = 'App Service Deployer'
    IsCustom = $true
    Description = 'Deploy and configure App Service only'
    Actions = @(
        'Microsoft.Web/sites/*',
        'Microsoft.Web/serverfarms/*',
        'Microsoft.Resources/deployments/*',
        'Microsoft.Resources/subscriptions/resourceGroups/read'
    )
    NotActions = @()
    DataActions = @()
    NotDataActions = @()
    AssignableScopes = @('/subscriptions/<sub-id>')
}
New-AzRoleDefinition -Role $deployOnlyRole
Auditing Contributor Assignments

Contributor at subscription scope is a significant access grant — review it carefully. Individual users (rather than groups) with Contributor, and service principals with Contributor at broad scopes, are common findings in access reviews that need remediation.

# Audit all Contributor assignments
Get-AzRoleAssignment -RoleDefinitionName 'Contributor' |
    Select-Object DisplayName, ObjectType, Scope | Sort-Object Scope | Format-Table

# Red flags:
# - Contributor at subscription scope (prefer resource group scope)
# - Individual users with Contributor (prefer groups)
# - Service principals with Contributor at subscription scope

Get-AzRoleAssignment -RoleDefinitionName 'Contributor' |
    Where-Object {
        $_.Scope -match '^/subscriptions/[^/]+$'  # subscription-level only
    } | Select-Object DisplayName, ObjectType, Scope

# Check which groups are Contributors (then audit group membership)
Get-AzRoleAssignment -RoleDefinitionName 'Contributor' |
    Where-Object { $_.ObjectType -eq 'Group' } |
    ForEach-Object {
        $members = Get-MgGroupMember -GroupId $_.ObjectId
        [PSCustomObject]@{
            GroupName = $_.DisplayName
            Scope = $_.Scope
            MemberCount = $members.Count
        }
    } | Format-Table
Resource-Specific Contributor Roles

The broad Contributor role (Actions: *) is Azure's convenience option. Azure also ships over 80 resource-specific Contributor variants that scope the same 'create and manage everything' semantics to a single resource provider. Virtual Machine Contributor, Network Contributor, and SQL DB Contributor are examples. These are more precise alternatives to the broad role and should be preferred when a team or automation only touches one class of resource.

# Azure has 100+ resource-specific Contributor built-in roles.
# These grant all operations for ONE resource provider rather than all providers.

# List all built-in Contributor variants
Get-AzRoleDefinition | Where-Object {
    $_.Name -like '*Contributor*' -and $_.IsCustom -eq $false
} | Select-Object Name | Sort-Object Name | Format-Table

# Examples of resource-specific Contributor roles:
# Virtual Machine Contributor       - VMs only, no networking or storage management
# Network Contributor               - All network resources
# Storage Account Contributor       - Storage accounts and keys
# SQL DB Contributor                - SQL databases (no security policies)
# Web Plan Contributor              - App Service Plans
# Key Vault Contributor             - Key Vault management (NOT secret/key access)
# Cosmos DB Account Reader Role     - read-only Cosmos
# App Configuration Data Owner      - DataActions for App Config

# Assign Virtual Machine Contributor to ops team (cannot touch networking/storage)
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId '<ops-group-id>' `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Virtual Machine Contributor' `
  -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/prod-compute-rg'

# Inspect what Virtual Machine Contributor can and cannot do
Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name 'Virtual Machine Contributor' |
    Select-Object -ExpandProperty Actions | Sort-Object
Resource-Specific Role
Allows
Blocks
Virtual Machine Contributor
All VM operations (start/stop/resize/delete/configure)
Networking, storage account management, RBAC
Network Contributor
All virtual networks, subnets, NSGs, route tables, load balancers
VMs, storage, RBAC
Storage Account Contributor
Create/manage storage accounts, list keys
Data plane (blob/queue/table content)
SQL DB Contributor
Create/manage SQL databases, configure settings
Security policies, role assignments, data reads
Key Vault Contributor
Create/configure Key Vaults (access policies in old model)
Secret/key/cert data plane access

Key Vault Contributor allows managing vault settings but not reading secrets. This is intentional — it enforces separation between vault administration and secret access. Add Key Vault Secrets User or Key Vault Reader for data plane access.
Contributor and Data Plane Access

A common misconception is that Contributor grants full access to everything inside a resource. It does not. Contributor's Actions: * covers only the control plane (the Azure Resource Manager layer — creating, configuring, and deleting resources). Data plane operations — reading blob content, getting Key Vault secrets, querying Cosmos DB documents — require separate DataActions roles even if the principal already holds Contributor on the resource.

# Contributor does NOT grant data plane access.
# Example: a Contributor on a storage account can manage the account
# but CANNOT read blob content without a separate DataActions role.

# Test: assign Contributor to a service principal on a storage account
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId $sp.Id `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Contributor' `
  -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/data-rg/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/proddata'

# The SP can list containers (control plane):
# az storage container list --account-name proddata --auth-mode login  -> SUCCESS

# But cannot download a blob (data plane):
# az storage blob download --container-name logs --name app.log --auth-mode login  -> 403

# Add blob data access:
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId $sp.Id `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Storage Blob Data Reader' `
  -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/data-rg/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/proddata'

# Same pattern for Key Vault:
# Contributor can manage vault settings but cannot get secrets — need 'Key Vault Secrets User'
New-AzRoleAssignment `
  -ObjectId $sp.Id `
  -RoleDefinitionName 'Key Vault Secrets User' `
  -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/data-rg/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/prod-kv'
Control plane (Actions): manage the resource itself — create containers, change settings, view metadata.
Data plane (DataActions): access the data inside the resource — download blobs, read secrets, execute queries.
These are evaluated by entirely separate authorization systems. Contributor provides no DataActions.

Adding a DataActions role on top of Contributor is correct and common: CI/CD pipeline as Contributor (can manage the storage account) plus Storage Blob Data Contributor (can read/write blob content for the app).
Narrowing from Contributor Over Time

Starting with the broad Contributor role is pragmatic during onboarding — the team does not yet know exactly which resource types they will use. After a few months, access reviews reveal the actual usage patterns, and you can narrow the assignment to resource-specific roles. This iterative refinement moves the team toward least privilege without blocking them at the outset.

# Pattern: start broad (Contributor on RG) and narrow as requirements clarify

# Month 1: team onboarded, assign Contributor at RG scope
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $teamGroupId -RoleDefinitionName 'Contributor' `
    -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/app-rg'

# Month 3: access review reveals team only touches App Service and SQL
# Remove broad Contributor
Remove-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $teamGroupId -RoleDefinitionName 'Contributor' `
    -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/app-rg'

# Add specific roles
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $teamGroupId -RoleDefinitionName 'Web Plan Contributor' `
    -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/app-rg'
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $teamGroupId -RoleDefinitionName 'Website Contributor' `
    -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/app-rg'
New-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $teamGroupId -RoleDefinitionName 'SQL DB Contributor' `
    -Scope '/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/app-rg'

# Check the resulting permissions are equivalent (for the resource types they use)
Get-AzRoleAssignment -ObjectId $teamGroupId | Select-Object RoleDefinitionName, Scope
Summary
Contributor is the go-to role for teams and automation that build and operate Azure resources without managing access control:
Actions: * with 6 NotActions. Everything Owner can do except create/modify role assignments, role definitions, resource locks, and Blueprint assignments.
The single practical difference from Owner is the inability to assign roles or manage Microsoft.Authorization resources.
Assign at resource group scope for application teams; subscription scope only for platform teams managing the whole subscription.
Contributor cannot create resource locks — use a custom role or grant User Access Administrator at the specific resource if lock management is needed.
For CI/CD pipelines, pair Contributor with federated credentials (OIDC) to eliminate stored secrets.
Regularly audit Contributor assignments at subscription scope — individual user or broad service principal assignments at that level are candidates for scope reduction.
